title: What Works on Wall Street
subtitle: The Classic Guide to the Best-Performing Investment Strategies of All Time
author: James P. O'Shaughnessy
description: The definitive quantitative investing guide. O'Shaughnessy analyzed 85 years of US stock market data — testing over a dozen investment factors across millions of data points — to identify which strategies actually outperformed and for how long.
keyIdeas: The Great Database: 85+ Years of US Equity Returns
The Market Value Effect: Small-Cap Outperformance
Why Price-to-Sales Beats P/E, P/B, and P/CF
Value vs. Growth: Value Wins Over the Long Run
Limitations of the Efficient Market Hypothesis
Strategy Decay and the Transience of Alpha
Process Over Strategy: Discipline Beats Ideas
keyTakeaways: The most reliable stock market outperformer tested across 85 years is small-cap value — it beat every other strategy tested.
Price-to-sales (P/S) is the single most effective standalone valuation metric, outperforming P/E, P/B, and dividend yield in long-term backtests.
The efficient market hypothesis is not fully supported by evidence: systematic value and small-cap factors generate persistent excess returns.
Most investment strategies degrade over time due to crowding and arbitrage — decay is the rule, not the exception.
The core-satellite approach (broad market index core + targeted factor-based satellite) captures the best of both active and passive investing.
Combining small size with value characteristics produces returns superior to either factor alone — the combination is synergistic.
Growth stocks underperform value stocks over the full market cycle, despite their appeal during late-stage bull markets.
You cannot beat the market by reading headlines or chasing momentum; only systematic, data-driven, disciplined processes produce consistent alpha.
Even a perfectly backtested strategy will eventually decay as more capital flows into it — managing expectations is as important as finding alpha.
Disciplined execution matters more than any single strategy; process and risk management drive long-term success.
The market rewards patience: strategies that look mediocre over 5 years often dominate across full market cycles including drawdowns.
Avoid the most expensive growth stocks at the peak of their popularity; valuation and momentum are inversely correlated to future returns.
whoShouldRead: Active investors and financial advisors interested in quantitative, evidence-based investing
Academics and students of finance who want to understand the empirical basis of factor investing
Index fund enthusiasts skeptical of active management who want to understand what the data actually shows
Anyone seeking to understand why value and small-cap stocks consistently outperform over full market cycles
Financial planners looking to build core-satellite portfolios using empirically validated factor tilts
